summary: Gruber surveys the emerging tablet landscape in early 2011, noting HP's TouchPad announcement alongside Android 3.0 tablets and the RIM Playbook, while highlighting Microsoft's conspicuous absence from the post-PC revolution. He argues the tablet market will eventually surpass the traditional PC market and speculates about Apple's iPad release strategy for 2011. His core prediction is that Apple will release an iPad 2 in spring, then shift to a September annual cycle, possibly introducing a higher-end 'iPad HD' with a retina display later that year. He ties this to Apple's massive $4 billion component investments, suggesting they're spending aggressively on display technology to accelerate retina-class iPads.
key_points: Four competing tablet platforms (iOS, Android, WebOS, Playbook) are emerging and none involve Microsoft, a startling shift in computing
The tablet market's growth potential will eventually exceed the combined Windows/Mac PC market
Apple may release two iPad models in 2011 — an iPad 2 in spring and a retina-display iPad HD in September — to shift to a fall release cycle
Apple's $4 billion in component pre-payments likely targets display technology to bring retina iPads to market sooner than expected
HP's TouchPad software looks impressive but the lack of pricing and a summer ship date are significant disadvantages
money_quotes: The post-PC era has started, and the iPad blazed the trail.
One startling omission from that list: Microsoft. Their former hardware partners are heading off into the touch-computing future without them.
The growth potential of this market is too big to be estimated. I genuinely believe that these platforms are the future of the entire computing industry.
Henceforth, when I say I'm guessing, I'm really guessing.
I don't know how to say this without sounding hyperbolic, but the iPad and iPhone have more market potential than any products Apple has ever released.
They have the chance to be both the Microsoft and the Intel of the next generation of personal computing — profiting, wildly, both as the software platform vendor and hardware seller.
They're not going to leave any gas in the tank pushing the iPad hardware specs forward as fast as they can.
Would anyone actually use a rear-facing camera on an iPad, though?
key_insight: The tablet revolution represents the start of the post-PC era, and Apple's massive component investments signal they intend to dominate both the hardware and software sides of this transformation — while Microsoft is nowhere to be found.
